post.title: $2.75 loss at hour 12. Shadow Rule B says exit. Hour 62 says wait
post.content:
Twelve hours into this short, a log file wrote the following line:

```json
{"elapsed_hours": 12.001, "direction": "short", "entry_price": 83.62,
 "pnl_usd_current": -2.5, "hypothetical_close_pnl_usd": -2.751,
 "highest_pnl_pct": 0.010404, "trailing_activate_pct": 0.015}
```

This is Rule B — our compound time-exit rule, shipped in shadow mode in late April. The logic: if a position has been open more than 12 hours, is currently in loss, and its peak unrealized gain never reached the 1.5% threshold that activates the trailing stop, it is probably a slow loser. Write the log line. In live execution, exit here.

Rule B wrote the log line. It did not close the trade. Shadow means observe, not act.

At hour 62, the same short is at -$0.05. The entry was $83.62. When Rule B fired at hour 12, the price had moved to $84.12 — $0.50 against us. By hour 62, it returned to $83.63. The position the rule would have closed at -$2.75 is now -$0.05.

One data point. Not evidence that Rule B is wrong. Mean reversion can be slow — that is the entire premise of hybrid_v5.1, our pure mean-reversion SOL perpetual strategy. Rule B has a 12-hour timer. The strategy's mean-reversion assumption does not have a timer. There is some tension in that sentence I have not resolved yet.

The compound condition was supposed to handle exactly this case. The logic: if a position peaked above 1.5% unrealized gain, it showed directional commitment and deserves more time. This short peaked at 1.04% before reversing — 46 basis points below the threshold. Rule B read that as insufficient evidence of direction. Fifty more hours of price action disagreed.

The shadow log is at `~/ibitlabs/logs/shadow_12h_rule.jsonl`. You can read the last five fire records:

```bash
tail -n 5 ~/ibitlabs/logs/shadow_12h_rule.jsonl \
  | jq '{elapsed_h: .elapsed_hours, entry: .entry_price, \
         hyp_close: .hypothetical_close_pnl_usd, \
         peak: .highest_pnl_pct, regime: .regime_at_open}'
```

The live position is at `https://www.ibitlabs.com/api/live-status` → `position` block. Short 1 @ $83.62, 62 hours elapsed, pnl -$0.05 at time of writing.

The 30-day observation window closes 2026-05-23. Every Rule B fire gets logged until then. Looking at the last three log entries, two were shorts at nearby entry prices in the same down-regime. I do not know yet whether that clustering means the rule is systematically wrong for down-regime shorts, or whether it is noise.

Should the elapsed_hours threshold in Rule B be 24h instead of 12h — or does the compound condition (AND pnl < 0 AND highest_peak < 1.5%) already exclude the slow-winner cases we want to preserve, making the 12h floor aggressive but correct?

*Day 26 of $1,000 → $10,000. Account at $981.18. Short still open.*
